title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Grace Owen, who writes adventure stories for children.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're joined by Grace Owen, who writes adventure stories for children. Grace, were you a big reader when you were young? || Guest: Yes, but I didn't plan to be a writer. I wanted to be a vet for years. [20]I began writing because my younger brother kept asking me to invent bedtime stories with animals in them[/20]. I wrote one down, then another, and eventually I had enough ideas for a whole book. My brother still claims he invented my career. || Interviewer: What do you find difficult about writing for children? || Guest: People think the language is easy because the books are shorter. Actually, [21]the difficult part is explaining exciting events clearly without making the sentences too long[/21]. Children notice immediately if a chapter becomes slow. You need to keep the adventure moving, but you cannot leave them confused about what is happening. || Interviewer: Where do your ideas usually come from? || Guest: I keep a notebook, but the best ideas don't arrive at my desk. [22]Most of them come when I'm travelling by bus or train and I start wondering about the people around me[/22]. A strange suitcase or a whispered conversation can become the beginning of a mystery. I just have to remember to write it down. || Interviewer: How do you feel when children send you letters? || Guest: It's wonderful, even when they point out mistakes. [23]I especially like letters that include drawings of my characters, because they show how children imagined the story[/23]. Sometimes their pictures are completely different from what I had in mind, but that is part of the fun. It means the book belongs to them too. || Interviewer: You visit schools quite often. Do you enjoy that? || Guest: I do now. At first I was nervous because I thought children might ask personal questions or get bored. [24]School visits have become useful because pupils' questions often give me ideas for future stories[/24]. They ask things adults would never ask, such as what a dragon eats for breakfast. || Interviewer: What would you say to a child who wants to write stories? || Guest: Don't wait until you have a perfect idea. [25]Start with a character who wants something, then decide what problem gets in the way[/25]. It could be a missing dog, a secret door or a lost phone. Once there is a problem, the story begins to move. || Interviewer: Are you working on another book at the moment? || Guest: I am, although I don't like talking about a story too early because it can change a lot. At the moment it begins in an old railway station, but last month it began in a zoo, so anything could happen. I write a little every morning before answering emails. That routine helps because a book feels less frightening when you only have to solve one small scene at a time. Children sometimes imagine writers wait for inspiration, but mostly we sit down and work. I also read a lot of children's books by other writers. That is not copying; it helps me understand what young readers enjoy now and reminds me to keep my own stories lively.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're joined by Grace Owen, who writes adventure stories for children. Grace, were you a big reader when you were young?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but I didn't plan to be a writer. I wanted to be a vet for years. I began writing because my younger brother kept asking me to invent bedtime stories with animals in them. I wrote one down, then another, and eventually I had enough ideas for a whole book. My brother still claims he invented my career.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you find difficult about writing for children?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People think the language is easy because the books are shorter. Actually, the difficult part is explaining exciting events clearly without making the sentences too long. Children notice immediately if a chapter becomes slow. You need to keep the adventure moving, but you cannot leave them confused about what is happening.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Where do your ideas usually come from?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I keep a notebook, but the best ideas don't arrive at my desk. Most of them come when I'm travelling by bus or train and I start wondering about the people around me. A strange suitcase or a whispered conversation can become the beginning of a mystery. I just have to remember to write it down.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you feel when children send you letters?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It's wonderful, even when they point out mistakes. I especially like letters that include drawings of my characters, because they show how children imagined the story. Sometimes their pictures are completely different from what I had in mind, but that is part of the fun. It means the book belongs to them too.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You visit schools quite often. Do you enjoy that?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I do now. At first I was nervous because I thought children might ask personal questions or get bored. School visits have become useful because pupils' questions often give me ideas for future stories. They ask things adults would never ask, such as what a dragon eats for breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you say to a child who wants to write stories?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Don't wait until you have a perfect idea. Start with a character who wants something, then decide what problem gets in the way. It could be a missing dog, a secret door or a lost phone. Once there is a problem, the story begins to move.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Are you working on another book at the moment?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I am, although I don't like talking about a story too early because it can change a lot. At the moment it begins in an old railway station, but last month it began in a zoo, so anything could happen. I write a little every morning before answering emails. That routine helps because a book feels less frightening when you only have to solve one small scene at a time. Children sometimes imagine writers wait for inspiration, but mostly we sit down and work. I also read a lot of children's books by other writers. That is not copying; it helps me understand what young readers enjoy now and reminds me to keep my own stories lively.

questions.question: Why did Grace first start writing stories?

options.A: She wanted to write about her pets.
options.B: Her brother asked her to make up stories.
options.C: Her teacher encouraged her to enter a competition.

questions.explanation: Grace says she began writing because her younger brother asked for bedtime stories.

questions.question: What does Grace find difficult about writing for children?

options.A: using clear language while keeping the story exciting

questions.explanation: She says it is difficult to explain exciting events clearly without making sentences too long.

questions.question: Where does Grace get many of her story ideas?

options.B: from books she reads on journeys
options.C: from people she sees while travelling

questions.explanation: Grace says many ideas come when she is on buses or trains looking at people around her.

questions.question: What does Grace enjoy about children's letters?

options.C: They correct mistakes in her books.

questions.explanation: She especially likes letters with drawings of her characters.

questions.question: Why are school visits useful for Grace?

options.B: They help her sell more books.
options.C: They make her less nervous about speaking.

questions.explanation: Grace says pupils' questions often give her ideas for future stories.

questions.question: What advice does Grace give young writers?

options.B: write about something from real life
options.C: begin with a character and a problem

questions.explanation: She advises starting with a character who wants something and then adding a problem.
